title: The Software Engineer's Guidebook
author: Gergely Orosz
subtitle: Navigating Senior, Tech Lead, and Staff Engineer Roles at Tech Companies and Startups
description: A practical, opinionated guide to the engineering career ladder at modern tech companies and well-funded startups. Drawing on interviews with hundreds of senior engineers, tech leads, staff engineers, and engineering managers at companies like Google, Meta, Stripe, Shopify, and high-growth startups, Gergely Orosz maps the expectations, scope of impact, and unwritten rules of each step from senior engineer through tech lead to staff engineer and beyond. The book covers performance reviews, promotions, scope expansion, technical leadership, the IC-versus-manager fork, salary negotiation, and how to operate in both big-tech and startup environments.
keyIdeas: The Engineering Career Ladder Is a Series of Scope Expansions
Senior Engineer: Ownership of Projects and Project-Level Scope
Tech Lead: Combining Hands-On Coding With Team-Level Scope
Staff Engineer: Cross-Team Scope Without Formal Authority
The Manager Versus IC Fork Is Real and Often Permanent
Big Tech and Startups Reward Different Behaviors
Scope of Impact Beats Raw Output at Senior Levels
Performance Reviews Are an Exercise in Self-Marketing
Promotions Require Evidence, Not Just Effort
Technical Leadership Is Influencing Without Authority
Writing and Articulating Are the Staff-Plus Multiplier
Salary Bands and Levers Differ Between Big Tech and Startups
keyTakeaways:
Every level up the engineering ladder expands scope: from
individual code, to project, to team, to cross-team, to organization

A senior engineer owns projects end to end — they define what
success looks like and drive it without being asked

A tech lead is a working lead: they still write substantial code
while orchestrating a team's technical direction

A staff engineer operates at team-of-teams scope, setting
technical direction across multiple teams without people-management
authority

Influence without authority is the central skill of staff-plus
engineers; it is built through clarity, relationships, and shipped work

The fork from individual contributor to engineering manager is
real and rarely reversible — choose deliberately, not by drift

Big tech rewards specialization, process, and visible cross-team
impact; startups reward generalism, speed, and shipped product

Scope of impact matters more than line count or commit count once
you are past the senior level

Calibration and self-promotion are not optional — they are how
the organization sees your work

Promotions require a portfolio of artifacts: design docs,
incidents handled, mentorship, and the narrative that ties them
together

Writing — design docs, RFCs, postmortems, vision memos — is the
single highest-leverage skill for staff-and-beyond engineers

Compensation is mostly market-driven; total rewards (equity,
bonus, leveling) is the lever to understand before negotiating
